text: The surgeon creates a large surgical flap across the midline during the robotic tap bilateral inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon creates one large flap across the midline, detaching the umbilical ligaments to improve visualization during the robotic tap bilateral inguinal hernia repair.
The surgeon proceeds straight down the midline, aiming to reach the pubis and Cooper's ligament, preparing the surgical field for hernia repair.
After creating a large flap across the midline, the surgeon proceeds to dissect laterally, extending the surgical exposure.
The surgeon maintains dissection within the true preperitoneal plane, carefully preserving the transversalis fascia during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon moves laterally within the preperitoneal plane to visualize the chord structures, maintaining the transfer cells fascia elevated.
The hernia is visible medial to the epigastric vessels, with surrounding anatomical structures coming into view.
The surgeon begins parietalizing the cord structures, noting the absence of an indirect hernia sac.
The surgeon targets the obturator fat plug, visually identifying and maneuvering around this structure during the hernia procedure.
The surgeon continues to parietalize the cord, separating it from surrounding tissues in the hernia repair procedure.
Two 12x16 meshes are visible in the abdomen, placed at the start of the hernia operation.
The surgeon searches for a lipoma within the surgical field and identifies a small lipoma amidst the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon inspects the surgical area to ensure there is no additional fat protruding into the hernia site.
The surgeon reduces the fat laterally, ensuring it extends beyond the inferior lateral edge of the mesh without amputating the fatty tissue.
The surgeon visually identifies a direct defect in the hernia on the left-hand side.
Visible sutures from a previous hernia repair are observed in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully continues to remove the recurrent direct hernia sac from the surgical field, using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon removes fat tissue over the femoral area to check for any hidden hernia.
The surgeon performs dissection down towards the obturator space on the left side, mirroring the technique previously used on the right side.
The surgeon completes the process of varietalizing the spermatic cord on the left side, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon searches for a lipoma on the left side, examining the area for any visible fatty tissue masses.
The surgeon observes fatty tissue extending into the surgical area, potentially indicating the presence of a lipoma or other fatty structures.
The surgeon identifies posterior nodal tissue during the hernia procedure.
The surgeon identifies tissue covering the internal ring, examining its appearance and position.
The surgeon ceases further exploration after confirming the absence of additional tissue, noting the potential for bleeding from nodal tissue.
The surgeon makes space for the inferior edge of the mesh while inspecting the visible hernia defects.
The surgeon places the meshes, overlapping them at the midline to cover the hernia defects.
The surgeon proceeds to close the flap, completing the coverage over the internal ring and hernia defects.
The surgeon begins sewing the mesh to the patient's left side, securing it in place over the hernia defect.
The surgeon places the lipoma back inside the flap on the right side and proceeds to close the defect on the right side of the flap.
